segments.title: Danish defences and the failed relief effort
segments.summary: The siege begins with Copenhagen's garrison, fortifications, and Wellesley's defeat of a Danish relief force.
segments.body: Copenhagen had roughly 10,000 defenders, split between regulars and militia, manning a broad fortified perimeter that included a major star fort. Most of the Danish fleet lay inside the harbour, where the defences should also have protected it. On 26 August, Wellesley's reserve and an artillery-heavy brigade defeated the force sent to relieve the city. The contrast introduces the episode's central puzzle: why a prepared defence produced so little effective resistance to the British expedition.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Danish garrison, its fortifications, and Wellesley's action against the relief force.

segments.title: The bombardment of Copenhagen
segments.summary: Britain's demand for the Danish fleet was followed by a four-day bombardment that used fire-setting rockets against a wooden city.
segments.body: After demanding the fleet, the British threatened Copenhagen with destruction and opened fire when Denmark refused. The bombardment continued from 2 to 5 September 1807, bringing sustained pressure against the capital. The account highlights Congreve rockets, whose incendiary effect was especially dangerous among wooden buildings and ships. British permission for civilians to leave reduced the number of people trapped in the city, but it did not prevent widespread material destruction.

evidence.note: The transcript states the British demand, the 2-5 September bombardment, civilian evacuation, and the incendiary use of Congreve rockets.

segments.title: Why did a strong defence fail so quickly?
segments.summary: The video asks why Britain faced so few losses against Copenhagen's extensive defences and trained Danish militia.
segments.body: The fortifications, garrison, and organized Danish militia should have imposed serious costs on an attacker. Instead, the British campaign achieved far more than the speaker considers likely from the initial military balance. He rejects a simple appeal to British exceptionalism, while acknowledging that luck and structural advantages could make British forces more effective than they appeared on paper. The two Copenhagen battles therefore become useful cases for asking how institutions, preparation, and opportunity combine to produce an unexpectedly one-sided result.

evidence.note: The speaker contrasts the defences and militia with the unexpectedly low British losses and questions a simple exceptionalist explanation.

segments.title: Peymann's impossible fleet decision
segments.summary: Danish commander Henrik Ernst Peymann had to preserve Copenhagen and deny its fleet to Britain, but could achieve neither objective.
segments.body: Peymann's orders were to hold Copenhagen and, if necessary, burn the fleet rather than allow Britain to seize it. He accomplished neither task, but the speaker sees understandable reasons not to destroy ships that might still serve as bargaining leverage. Surrendering them could spare the city when the alternative was continued bombardment by a force with overwhelming firepower. Yet the episode also exposes unused options: a defended harbour fleet could have been dispersed, better secured, or sent to sea before British pressure hardened into blockade and attack.

evidence.note: The transcript names Peymann's duty to hold Copenhagen or burn the fleet and considers the choices he did not take.

segments.title: Evacuation and Copenhagen's civilian fire response
segments.summary: Copenhagen's advanced fire measures depended on civilians who had been allowed to leave before the bombardment.
segments.body: The city possessed comparatively advanced fire-fighting arrangements, but much of the manpower behind them was civilian. Evacuation therefore had a grim double effect: it protected many inhabitants from bombardment while leaving fewer people available to contain the fires. Once incendiaries took hold, the undermanned response could not prevent extensive damage across the wooden city. This watch point connects civilian protection, civil defence, and the physical vulnerability of an early nineteenth-century capital under siege.

evidence.note: The transcript explains that civilian manpower was essential to Copenhagen's fire-fighting measures.

segments.title: How the Royal Navy used the captured fleet
segments.summary: The captured Danish fleet was selectively altered, laid up, used, or discarded according to what the Royal Navy actually needed.
segments.body: British naval architects altered captured Danish ships to fit Royal Navy preferences, reducing Christian VII from 84 to 80 guns and increasing Crown Prince Frederick to 74. Ships still on the stocks were burned, while some captured gunboats sank during removal and were later refloated by Danish or Norwegian forces. Ships of the line offered a reserve against a longer or more costly war, but many were laid up and never became regular operational units. Frigates mattered more immediately because the Royal Navy was chronically short of the smaller cruising ships needed for escort, scouting, and overseas duties.

evidence.note: The transcript covers re-rating captured Danish ships, the limited use of ships of the line, and the greater value of frigates.

segments.title: Frigates, small ships, and usable sea power
segments.summary: Frigates made a navy effective beyond battle fleets by scouting, escorting convoys, training officers, and covering routine duties.
segments.body: The speaker calls frigates the eyes of the fleet and distinguishes a battle fleet from the vessels that perform its wider daily work. They escorted convoys, scouted, supported distant commitments, and provided commands in which officers could gain experience before handling larger ships. Frigates were also cheaper to mobilize than ships of the line and sufficient for many missions, while brigs offered further useful small-ship capacity. A comparison with modern fleet totals makes the same point: many smaller vessels can be strategically rational when their missions demand presence and utility rather than only capital-ship tonnage.

evidence.note: The transcript explains frigates' escort, scouting, training, and routine-service functions and compares the logic with modern fleets.

segments.title: The speaker's legal case for the strike
segments.summary: The speaker argues that French-Danish discussions over the Continental System gave Britain a legal basis for acting against the fleet.
segments.body: The video presents Denmark's interest in the Continental System as a survival calculation between British pressure and the expanding French Empire, not a simple morality tale. France posed the greater danger of territorial conquest, so Danish leaders had a rational reason to accommodate it. The fleet gave Denmark valuable leverage in negotiations with France, but its possible alignment with France was also exactly what alarmed Britain. On that basis, the speaker argues that French-Danish discussions and Denmark's leaning toward the system made the British strike lawful.

evidence.note: The transcript links Denmark's survival calculation and French-Danish discussions over the Continental System to the speaker's legal case for British action.

segments.title: Denmark's strategic paradox after losing the fleet
segments.summary: Losing the fleet perversely made it easier for Denmark to satisfy France without remaining a major naval threat to Britain.
segments.body: The speaker develops a deliberately cynical interpretation of Denmark's position after the attack. Denmark could enter the Continental System and thereby reduce the danger of French invasion, yet the captured fleet left it without the means to enforce that system effectively at sea. Britain, having removed the naval threat, no longer had the same reason to invade Denmark. The outcome did not make the bombardment harmless, but it illustrates how losing a strategic asset could narrow a smaller state's exposure to two competing great powers.

evidence.note: The transcript argues that Denmark could join the Continental System after Britain removed the fleet, reducing the immediate incentive for either power to invade.

segments.title: Removing a fleet threat and keeping sea lanes open
segments.summary: Britain's operational purpose was to neutralize the Danish fleet, not to prevent Denmark from joining France's alliance system.
segments.body: The speaker separates the expedition's practical naval objective from its political consequences. Attacking Denmark was unlikely to keep it out of an alliance promising protection from further attack. Britain instead needed to prevent the Danish fleet from becoming an active problem under French influence. Capturing that fleet removed the immediate threat and helped keep Britain's sea lanes open.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes stopping Danish alliance entry from the operational aim of neutralizing the fleet and preserving sea lanes.

segments.title: Why Copenhagening became a verb
segments.summary: The second Copenhagen operation illustrates a repeated British willingness to use pre-emptive naval force against a fleet left exposed.
segments.body: The name is treated as a shorthand for forceful action repeated around Copenhagen, not merely a label for a single battle. The Royal Navy's record in the region made another aggressive intervention a danger Danish planners should have anticipated. Denmark had concentrated much of its army against the French threat to the south, but its expensive rebuilt fleet remained exposed in Copenhagen. The speaker's practical critique is that the fleet could have been moved to a safer anchorage or put to sea in the Baltic rather than left for Britain to seize.

evidence.note: The speaker explains the repeated Copenhagen precedent and questions why Denmark left its rebuilt fleet insecure.

segments.title: Denmark caught between France and Britain
segments.summary: The closing analysis treats Denmark's response as a constrained attempt to avoid a wider Baltic war while caught between two great powers.
segments.body: The expedition helped produce war with Russia, yet the speaker argues that this consequence did not trouble Britain as much as the Danish fleet had. Trade between Denmark and Britain appears to have continued despite the crisis, while British evacuation of civilians limited deaths and one political cost of the bombardment. He rejects a conspiracy theory but argues that Danish leaders had to think pragmatically about their position between France and Britain. In that reading, the weak relief effort, the fleet's surrender, and Denmark's slow move into the Continental System reflect a state trying to survive an impossible strategic position rather than a coordinated bargain with Britain.

evidence.note: The transcript considers continued trade, civilian evacuation, the weak relief effort, and Denmark's constrained choices between France and Britain.
